digestIntro: The Southern Interior flood warnings are your most time-sensitive item today — if you have family or property near the Thompson or any tributary, check evacuation alerts now. On the policy front, immigration officials are being pressed hard on whether international student permits displace Canadian workers, which will shape the next wave of IRCC rules your university clients are already bracing for. Meanwhile, AI infrastructure spending is accelerating at a scale (SoftBank's €75B in France alone) that will redefine what "AI-powered marketing" means for your consulting pitch within 18 months.
whyItMatters: The Southern Interior flood warnings are your most time-sensitive item today — if you have family or property near the Thompson or any tributary, check evacuation alerts now. On the policy front, immigration officials are being pressed hard on whether international student permits displace Canadian workers, which will shape the next wave of IRCC rules your university clients are already bracing for. Meanwhile, AI infrastructure spending is accelerating at a scale (SoftBank's €75B in France alone) that will redefine what "AI-powered marketing" means for your consulting pitch within 18 months. And Kerry-Lynne Findlay's BC Conservative leadership win sets up a genuine contest against Eby's NDP — provincial policy on housing, post-secondary funding, and short-term rentals could all shift direction.
audioScript:
Good morning, Midya. It's Sunday, May thirty-first, twenty twenty-six. Here's what matters today — and the lead item is one you should check before you do anything else.

Let's start local, because this is time-sensitive. The BC River Forecast Centre has issued flood warnings and evacuation alerts for the Southern Interior, and that includes the Kamloops area. Warm weather has sharply accelerated snowmelt across the region, and river levels — including in areas that feed into Kamloops — are rising. The Vancouver Sun and CFJC Today both confirmed active alerts as of last night. If you have family near the Thompson or any of its tributaries, or if you have property at Sun Peaks where drainage could be affected, now is the time to check the provincial Emergency Info BC site and Kamloops city emergency updates. Do not wait on this one. These situations can escalate faster than the forecast suggests, especially heading into a warm weekend. This is family-safety first — everything else can wait a few minutes.

On a more optimistic local note, Kamloops City Council has approved a housing agreement for a supportive facility serving women and children. It's not a headline-grabbing decision, but it's a meaningful signal that the city is continuing to move forward on housing gaps even as the broader provincial affordability crisis grinds on. And speaking of the province —

Kerry-Lynne Findlay won the BC Conservative leadership race last night, narrowly, after four rounds of voting. She finished with fifty-one percent in the final round, beating out a crowded field that included Kamloops' own Mike Milobar, who finished fifth. Findlay is a former federal MP and a tough campaigner. She's now the official leader of the opposition to David Eby's NDP government, and she's already framing her agenda around what she called the NDP's "economic vandalism." For you, this matters on several levels: provincial housing policy, short-term rental regulation in BC, post-secondary funding, and the broader business climate that your consulting clients operate in — all of that could shift meaningfully depending on where Findlay takes the party in the lead-up to the next election. It's worth watching how she positions on post-secondary specifically, because Eby's government has made significant interventions there, and any reversal or softening could change the strategic calculus for BC's universities.

Moving to higher education, and there's real signal here for your consulting pipeline. Immigration officials were put on the defensive in what sounds like a pointed parliamentary grilling this week over a core question: are international students taking jobs from Canadian workers? This debate has been building for months as the federal government has tried to thread a needle — maintaining study permit revenue for institutions while responding to public pressure over housing and labour market impacts. The questioning, reported in Mshale and circulating widely, suggests MPs are not satisfied with the reassurances IRCC has been offering. That means more policy turbulence is likely coming — whether that's further cap reductions, tighter PGWP eligibility, or new sector restrictions. If you're advising universities on their international student strategy, the message is clear: the status quo is not stable. Institutions that get ahead of diversification — whether that's domestic recruitment, alternative credentials, or community partnerships — are going to be in a much better position than those waiting for the federal rules to settle.

There's a related story worth noting: a published opinion piece is calling on governments to crack down on predatory rental practices targeting international students. We're talking illegal subletting, inflated deposits, unsafe housing conditions — the kind of thing that drives students to forums and social media before they ever tell their institution. This is a reputation risk that universities often underestimate. If your consulting work touches student experience or institutional marketing, this is a thread worth pulling. Institutions that visibly champion student housing protections can differentiate meaningfully.

And there's a quieter but genuinely interesting story from CBC on Inuit students and the value of attending university closer to home. The piece highlights enrolment, retention, and cultural relevance as the key drivers — and implicitly makes the case that regional universities serving northern and Indigenous communities have a distinct and defensible positioning. If you're looking for Canadian institutions that might benefit from a focused brand strategy conversation, universities in the north or those actively building northern partnerships are worth a look. There's a real narrative opportunity there that most institutions haven't yet learned to tell well.

On the AI front, three things deserve your attention today, and they sit at very different scales.

At the macro end: SoftBank has committed to invest up to seventy-five billion euros in AI data centres in France. The goal is five gigawatts of new compute capacity. Let that sink in for a moment. This isn't a press release about exploring AI — this is one of the world's largest technology investors betting that sovereign AI infrastructure is a geopolitical asset. France is not the only country racing to lock up compute. Canada is in this race too, and we covered the sustainability questions around Vancouver's AI data centres in a related climate piece this week. For your consulting work, the strategic implication is this: the cost and availability of AI compute is going to be shaped by decisions being made right now, at a scale that individual companies and institutions cannot control. Your clients who are building AI-dependent marketing functions need to be thinking about compute costs as a strategic variable, not just a line item.

At the enterprise tool level: GitHub Copilot, Microsoft's AI coding assistant that has become standard equipment in a huge number of development teams, has switched to token-based billing. Developers are not happy. The backlash is loud and pointed. This matters beyond the developer community because it signals a broader shift: the AI tool vendors who spent the last two years acquiring users with flat-rate pricing are now moving toward consumption-based models. That trend will hit marketing AI tools next. If your clients are budgeting for AI tool subscriptions, those budgets need a risk buffer for repricing. The days of predictable, low-cost AI tool access are likely ending.

And at the consumer hardware end: Meta is reportedly developing an AI pendant — a wearable device that would complement the AI-powered Ray-Ban smart glasses they've already shipped. This is part of a deliberate hardware strategy from Meta: get AI into ambient, always-on form factors that sit outside the smartphone. For brand strategists and marketers, this is a five-year signal, not a tomorrow problem. But the question of how people interact with AI assistants — and therefore how brands reach people through those assistants — is going to be one of the defining strategic questions of your consulting practice. Starting to think about it now puts you ahead.

On the real estate front, two stories this weekend frame the structural picture well. Rates.ca published a deep look at why Canadian housing remains unaffordable in 2026 — specifically, they've traced a thirty-five-year disconnect between income growth and home price growth. The compounding nature of that gap is what makes it so stubborn. This isn't a short-cycle problem that lower interest rates will solve; it's a structural misalignment that has been building since the early nineties. For you, this is useful context whether you're tracking your own BC real estate positions, or whether you're in conversations with clients who operate in housing-adjacent sectors.

Across the border, US housing inventory has turned negative year-over-year for the first time in this cycle. Active listings just dropped below eight hundred thousand, which is fewer than were available at the same point last year, while mortgage rates are holding at six-point-five-six percent. Historically, when supply tips negative while rates stay elevated, you get a standoff: buyers can't afford to move, sellers won't list because they'd lose their locked-in rate, and the market freezes. Watch how this plays out in the US because it tends to be a leading indicator for Canadian sentiment, particularly in BC markets where American buyers and investor flows have an outsized influence.

Two global stories worth your attention this morning. First: the Iran nuclear deal situation has gotten more complicated overnight. Trump announced a framework deal on Friday — and then, according to senior administration officials speaking to Axios, sat down in the Situation Room and asked for several amendments. He wants stronger terms specifically around nuclear material disposal, which is the hardest part to renegotiate. The parties are now back in back-and-forth that could last several more days. The reason this matters to you as a senior leader watching the macro environment: an unresolved Iran deal keeps oil markets uncertain and sustains a broader sense of geopolitical instability that affects business confidence, interest rate outlooks, and investment flows. Not your immediate concern, but a variable to watch.

Second: Israeli troops have made their deepest incursion into Lebanon in twenty-six years, capturing a strategic mountain fortress — a Crusader-era castle — in the south. This is the furthest Israeli forces have advanced since 2000. It's a significant military escalation that suggests the conflict in the region is not de-escalating, despite ceasefire frameworks that have been discussed. Again, for a senior executive briefing: this is context for a world that remains materially less stable than it was two years ago, and that instability has downstream effects on everything from supply chains to currency markets.

Finally, two ASCO stories from STAT News that are worth your attention from a health literacy standpoint — ASCO being the world's largest oncology conference, currently underway.

First, BioNTech and Pfizer presented promising data on bispecific antibodies for lung cancer. Bispecifics are a class of immunotherapy that can target two different proteins simultaneously, giving them potential advantages over older single-target approaches. The data presented at ASCO Day 2 was described by STAT as genuinely promising, and FDA oncology chief Rick Pazdur used the moment to signal that the agency sees the current disruption — which has been significant, with staffing cuts and structural changes — as an opportunity to rebuild smarter. That's a notable statement from the person who controls the pace of cancer drug approvals in the United States.

Second: Revolution Medicines has begun shipping its experimental pancreatic cancer drug, daraxonrasib, to patients under early access, ahead of a formal FDA submission that the CEO says is coming soon. Pancreatic cancer has one of the worst survival rates of any cancer type, and RAS-targeted drugs have been a long-sought goal in oncology. This is a meaningful development — not a cure, but a genuine step forward in a disease that has resisted treatment for decades. Worth knowing about.

That's your Sunday briefing, Midya. The flood situation in the Southern Interior is the one to act on immediately — check in with family and monitor evacuation alerts before the day gets away from you. Beyond that, the through-line today is managed uncertainty: the IRCC policy environment for your university clients is unsettled and getting more so, AI infrastructure investment is locking in advantages at a scale that will reshape your industry, and BC's political landscape just shifted with Findlay's win in ways that will take months to fully play out. You're well-positioned to advise through all of it — the key is to keep translating these macro signals into concrete strategic conversations before your clients feel the pressure themselves. Stay ahead of it. Have a good Sunday.

topStories.headline: Flood Warnings and Rising Water Levels Hit the Kamloops Area
topStories.summary: The River Forecast Centre has issued flood warnings for BC's Southern Interior, including the Kamloops area, as warm weather accelerates snowmelt and pushes river levels higher — check local evacuation alerts if you're near any waterway.

topStories.headline: Kerry-Lynne Findlay Elected BC Conservative Leader After Four-Round Vote
topStories.summary: Findlay narrowly won with 51% in the final round, positioning the BC Conservatives to challenge Premier Eby's NDP in the next provincial election — a result that will shape BC's policy environment on housing, education, and short-term rentals.

topStories.headline: Kamloops City Council Adopts Housing Agreement for Women and Children's Facility
topStories.summary: Council approved a housing agreement for a supportive facility serving women and children, a small but meaningful step in Kamloops' ongoing effort to address shelter gaps.

digestStories.headline: Flood Warnings and Rising Water Levels Hit the Kamloops Area
digestStories.summary: The River Forecast Centre has issued flood warnings for BC's Southern Interior, including the Kamloops area, as warm weather accelerates snowmelt and pushes river levels higher — check local evacuation alerts if you're near any waterway.

digestStories.headline: Kerry-Lynne Findlay Elected BC Conservative Leader After Four-Round Vote
digestStories.summary: Findlay narrowly won with 51% in the final round, positioning the BC Conservatives to challenge Premier Eby's NDP in the next provincial election — a result that will shape BC's policy environment on housing, education, and short-term rentals.

digestStories.headline: Kamloops City Council Adopts Housing Agreement for Women and Children's Facility
digestStories.summary: Council approved a housing agreement for a supportive facility serving women and children, a small but meaningful step in Kamloops' ongoing effort to address shelter gaps.

digestStories.headline: Flood Warnings and Evacuation Alerts Issued for BC's Southern Interior
digestStories.summary: BC's River Forecast Centre warns of rising river levels across the Southern Interior due to accelerated snowmelt — evacuation alerts are active and conditions may escalate through the weekend.

digestStories.headline: Immigration Official Grilled: Are International Students Taking Canadian Jobs?
digestStories.summary: A parliamentary hearing put IRCC officials on the defensive over whether the volume of study permits has depressed wages and employment for Canadian-born workers — a debate that will directly shape permit caps and institutional quotas your university clients depend on.

digestStories.headline: Opinion: Canada Must Tackle Predatory Rental Practices Targeting International Students
digestStories.summary: A published opinion piece calls on federal and provincial governments to regulate landlords who exploit international students through illegal subletting, inflated deposits, and unsafe conditions — an issue that affects student retention and institutional reputation.

digestStories.headline: Why Attending University Closer to Home Could Be a Game-Changer for Inuit Students
digestStories.summary: A CBC report highlights the outsized impact of regional and northern universities on Inuit learners, pointing to enrolment, retention, and culturally relevant programming as key levers — a client signal for institutions seeking differentiated positioning.

digestStories.headline: SoftBank to Invest Up to €75 Billion in French AI Data Centres
digestStories.summary: SoftBank's commitment to build up to 5 gigawatts of data centre capacity in France signals that sovereign AI infrastructure is becoming a geopolitical and commercial imperative — the scale of compute being locked up will reshape AI access costs for every downstream user, including marketers.

digestStories.headline: GitHub Copilot Shifts to Token-Based Billing, Frustrating Developers
digestStories.summary: Microsoft's move from flat-rate to consumption-based pricing for Copilot has triggered significant developer backlash, signalling a broader industry shift toward metered AI tool costs that enterprise buyers — and consulting clients — will need to budget for differently.

digestStories.headline: Meta Reportedly Developing an AI-Powered Pendant Wearable
digestStories.summary: Meta is building on its Ray-Ban smart glasses momentum with a wearable AI pendant, pushing ambient, always-on AI closer to mainstream consumer reality — relevant to anyone building brand strategy around how people interact with AI assistants.

digestStories.headline: Why Canadian Housing Remains Unaffordable in 2026: A 35-Year Disconnect Explained
digestStories.summary: Rates.ca unpacks the structural gap between income growth and home prices that has compounded over three and a half decades, offering useful framing for anyone advising clients or making personal real estate decisions in BC's market.

digestStories.headline: US Housing Inventory Turns Negative Year-Over-Year for First Time
digestStories.summary: Active US listings dropped below year-ago levels — 795,921 vs. 803,479 — while mortgage rates hold at 6.56%, a supply signal that typically precedes renewed price pressure and has cross-border implications for Canadian market sentiment.

digestStories.headline: Trump Requests Amendments to Iran Nuclear Deal His Envoys Negotiated
digestStories.summary: After announcing a framework deal on Friday, Trump is pushing for stronger terms on nuclear material in a new round of back-and-forth with Tehran that could last days — an unresolved deal keeps oil markets and global risk sentiment on edge.

digestStories.headline: Israeli Army Makes Deepest Incursion into Lebanon in 26 Years, Captures Strategic Castle
digestStories.summary: Israeli troops seized a Crusader-era mountain fortress in southern Lebanon, marking the furthest territorial advance since 2000 and significantly escalating the regional military posture.

digestStories.headline: ASCO 2026, Day 2: BioNTech/Pfizer Bispecifics Show Promise; FDA's Pazdur Signals Restructuring Opportunity
digestStories.summary: STAT News from ASCO: promising bispecific antibody data for lung cancer and new immunotherapy findings were presented, while FDA oncology chief Rick Pazdur — acknowledging institutional disruption — framed the moment as a chance to rebuild the agency's cancer review process more boldly.

digestStories.headline: Revolution Medicines Begins Shipping Experimental Pancreatic Cancer Drug Ahead of FDA Submission
digestStories.summary: STAT News: Revolution Medicines is providing early access to daraxonrasib, a RAS-targeting drug for pancreatic cancer, with an FDA approval submission expected imminently — a significant development for one of oncology's hardest-to-treat cancers.

upNext.headline: Kerry-Lynne Findlay Elected BC Conservative Leader After Four-Round Vote
upNext.summary: Findlay narrowly won with 51% in the final round, positioning the BC Conservatives to challenge Premier Eby's NDP in the next provincial election — a result that will shape BC's policy environment on housing, education, and short-term rentals.
